title: Sheetz is quitting VMware, migrating 11,000 virtual machines
gist: US convenience store chain Sheetz is migrating roughly 11,000 virtual machines across all 838 of its stores off Broadcom's VMware vSphere onto StorMagic's SvHCI, citing Broadcom's subscription-only licensing, projected price hikes, and a mandatory five-year commitment as the deciding factors, joining Allstate, T-Mobile, and Tesco among enterprises leaving VMware amid a Gartner-estimated 35% workload migration by 2028.

insights.claim: Sheetz is migrating roughly 11,000 virtual machines across all 838 of its store locations from Broadcom's VMware vSphere to StorMagic's SvHCI, with over 600 stores already migrated at a pace of about 200 per month and full completion expected within about four months of the announcement.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: A concrete, in-progress large-scale VMware exit with a measurable migration velocity (200 stores/month) that can serve as a benchmark case study for the pace and feasibility of enterprise VMware displacement.

insights.claim: Sheetz infrastructure manager Scott Robertson said Broadcom's elimination of perpetual licenses in favor of mandatory subscription bundles with a five-year commitment, combined with projected price hikes, 'created too much uncertainty around long-term budgeting and increased our vendor dependence,' directly forcing the migration decision.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Direct customer-voiced evidence that Broadcom's post-acquisition VMware licensing strategy is actively driving large enterprise customers to competitors, a data point for assessing Broadcom's VMware revenue retention risk.
insights.quote: The projected price hikes, coupled with a mandatory subscription model and a five-year commitment, simply created too much uncertainty around long-term budgeting and increased our vendor dependence.

insights.claim: Gartner estimated in September that 35 percent of VMware workloads will migrate to other platforms by 2028.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: A market-sizing figure (35% of workloads migrating by 2028) that is directly usable for modeling Broadcom's VMware segment revenue retention risk versus the bull case that its licensing changes are 'financially successful.'

insights.claim: StorMagic, historically known for serving small and midsize businesses, is explicitly positioning itself to win Broadcom's large distributed enterprise customers by arguing that 'edge' locations of big enterprises (retail, grocery, branch offices) face IT constraints similar to SMB datacenters — limited space, power, on-site staff, and budget.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Identifies StorMagic as a direct commercial beneficiary of VMware/Broadcom customer defection in the specific niche of large, geographically distributed retail-style enterprises — a competitive name to track against Broadcom's edge-virtualization revenue.
insights.quote: Historically, large enterprises tolerated the 'VMware tax' at their edge locations because it was the status quo. However, with recent massive industry shifts, specifically Broadcom's acquisition of VMware, enterprises are facing massive budget increases just to keep their remote sites running
insights.locator: quote from StorMagic SVP Scott Mann

insights.claim: Other major enterprises reported to be migrating off VMware include insurer Allstate, telecom T-Mobile, and UK grocery chain Tesco, indicating the Broadcom-driven exodus extends across multiple industries beyond retail convenience chains.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Corroborates that VMware attrition is a broad, cross-industry trend rather than an isolated case, strengthening the signal for Broadcom VMware-segment revenue risk monitoring.
insights.locator: paragraph listing other enterprises leaving VMware

insights.claim: Broadcom has publicly maintained that its changes to VMware's licensing model are consistent with the rest of the industry and that its acquisition of VMware is considered financially successful.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Balances the customer-attrition narrative with Broadcom's own framing, which investors should weigh against concrete defection data points (Sheetz, Allstate, T-Mobile, Tesco, Gartner's 35% estimate) when assessing whether the licensing strategy is truly net-positive for Broadcom's software revenue.
